# MACRO REPORT — 2026-04-25
**Delivered:** 11:26 UTC | **Generated by:** Claude claude-sonnet-4-6

---

## EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

The dominant macro narrative entering the weekend is a war-driven energy shock centred on the US-Iran conflict, which has pushed Brent crude above $105/bbl, reignited inflation expectations, and materially delayed Federal Reserve rate-cutting timelines. A Reuters poll of 103 economists now places the first Fed cut no earlier than Q4 2026, with PCE inflation forecast at 3.7% in Q2 — the most worrying US inflation picture in nearly four years [Source: https://www.kitco.com/news/off-the-wire/2026-04-22/fed-rate-cut-pushed-back-late-2026-war-related-inflation-risks]. The single most important variable for today's session is the trajectory of US-Iran peace talks and any Strait of Hormuz developments, which act as the primary driver of oil prices, inflation expectations, yields, the dollar, and risk sentiment simultaneously. Equities have managed to hold near records thanks to a Trump ceasefire extension and a blowout earnings season, but the oil-inflation-rates feedback loop remains the key tail risk [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/markets/stocks/live/stock-market-today-wednesday-april-22-dow-sp-500-nasdaq-trump-us-iran-ceasefire-230429476.html].

---

## 1. GLOBAL MACRO OVERVIEW

US equity markets closed mixed-to-higher into the end of the week, with the S&P 500 at 7,165.08 (+0.8%) and the Nasdaq 100 at 27,303.67 (+1.95%) scoring fresh record closes aided by a strong earnings season — most notably Intel's double-digit pop on Q1 results, Tesla's earnings beat at $0.41 EPS vs. $0.37 expected, and P&G's solid Q3. The Dow Jones, however, underperformed at 49,230.71 (-0.16%), reflecting ongoing rotation into growth and tech amid the wartime backdrop [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/^GSPC]. VIX fell 3.11% to 18.71, indicating reduced near-term fear, though it remains elevated versus pre-conflict levels [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/^VIX].

The inflation backdrop is the most challenging since 2022. US CPI stands at 3.32% YoY as of March [Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CPIAUCSL], and PPI MoM is running at 1.78% [Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/PPIACO]. S&P Global's flash composite PMI showed service sector activity recovering to 51.3 in April from a three-year low of 49.8, but the survey flagged the worst inflation picture in nearly four years as companies scramble to secure scarce supplies — directly echoing pandemic-era dynamics [Source: https://www.marketwatch.com/story/u-s-inflation-picture-is-the-worst-in-almost-4-years-f3a3b03a]. PCE inflation is now forecast at 3.7% in Q2 2026, approximately 30 basis points above late-March estimates.

Federal Reserve policy is firmly on hold. The effective Fed Funds rate sits at 3.64% [Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/FEDFUNDS], with markets now pricing no cuts before late 2026. Fed Chair nominee Kevin Warsh's Senate confirmation hearing this week reinforced this view — his comment that "we have a short window to try to bring inflation back down" was interpreted by some economists as tilting toward hikes rather than cuts [Source: https://www.sfchronicle.com/news/politics/article/don-t-count-on-rate-cuts-just-yet-warsh-as-fed-22225603.php]. Treasury Secretary Bessent provided political cover for an extended hold. The 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.34% and the 2-year at 3.83%, maintaining a modestly positive curve [Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/DGS10].

Natural gas storage inventories printed a healthy build of 103 Bcf for the week ending April 17, taking working gas to 2,063 Bcf — 137 Bcf above the five-year average — which caps upside in domestic Henry Hub gas prices even as the Iran conflict keeps oil elevated [Source: https://www.oilandgas360.com/weekly-gas-storage-04-17/]. Meanwhile, Japan's core CPI accelerated for the first time in five months to 1.8% in March as the Iran war pushes energy import costs higher, complicating Bank of Japan policy normalisation [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/24/japan-cpi-march-inflation-iran-war-boj-rate.html].

The week ahead brings the critical Mag-7 earnings test, with Microsoft, Meta, Amazon, and Apple all due to report. Markets enter this event with elevated expectations, and any disappointment from these mega-cap names — combined with persistent oil-driven inflation — could rapidly reverse the recent equity-driven optimism. The macro backdrop remains a precarious balancing act: record equity prices, a constrained Fed, and a geopolitical flashpoint in the Middle East that is the master variable for virtually every major asset class.

---

## 2. CONFLICT / GEOPOLITICAL STATUS

**US-Iran War — Active Conflict, Ceasefire Fragile**

- President Trump extended the US-Iran ceasefire indefinitely on April 22 to allow for further peace talks, but it remained unclear whether Iran or Israel had formally agreed to the extension, keeping markets on edge [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/markets/stocks/live/stock-market-today-wednesday-april-22-dow-sp-500-nasdaq-trump-us-iran-ceasefire-230429476.html].
- Brent crude climbed approximately 12% on the week — its biggest weekly gain since early March when the war began — as stalled peace talks and Strait of Hormuz supply disruption fears drove the rally, with front-month futures trading above $107/bbl during Friday's session [Source: https://www.wsj.com/livecoverage/stock-market-today-dow-sp-500-nasdaq-04-24-2026].
- A second round of US-Iran diplomatic talks is expected, with Iran's Foreign Minister potentially arriving in Pakistan as an intermediary; Goldman Sachs' Jared Cohen stated "The Strait of Hormuz will never reopen the way it was at the beginning" [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/video/2026/04/24/final-march-consumer-sentiment-comes-in-at-49-point-8-above-estimates.html].
- US oil exports are set to hit record highs as producers capitalise on elevated prices, and the US recently seized an Iranian vessel near the Strait of Hormuz, escalating maritime tensions [Source: https://oilprice.com/Energy/Crude-Oil/StanChart-95-Per-Barrel-Is-The-New-Oil-Price-Equilibrium.html].
- The Iran conflict is adding to inflationary pressures globally; South Africa's central bank warned the war would push up prices, and Turkey's central bank held rates on war uncertainty [Source: https://www.kitco.com/news/off-the-wire/2026-04-22/stocks-fx-dip-us-iran-ceasefire-uncertainty-focus-cenbank-decisions].
- US gas prices now range from $3.38/gallon in Oklahoma to $5.82/gallon in California, with the Iran war cited as the primary driver of the surge [Source: https://www.washingtonpost.com/business/2026/04/23/gas-prices-lowest-highest-by-state/].

### Disputed

- [Report states the S&P Global services sector PMI recovered to 51.3 "in April" from a three-year low of 49.8 "in the prior month"]: The MarketWatch source says "S&P Global's index of service companies…recovered to a reading of 51.3 in April from a three-year low of 49.8 in the prior month." However, the CNBC consumer sentiment article headline reads "Final March consumer sentiment comes in at 49.8" — this refers to the University of Michigan consumer sentiment reading, not the S&P Global services PMI. These are two distinct series. The report conflates these without clear attribution, creating potential confusion, though the MarketWatch source does explicitly support the 51.3/49.8 services PMI figures.
- [Report attributes Brent's ~12% weekly rally specifically to "its biggest weekly gain since early March when the war began"]: The WSJ source states "Brent crude has climbed roughly 12%, putting it on pace for its biggest weekly gain since early March, shortly after the war began." This is confirmed, but the data bundle shows the settlement price for Brent (BZ=F) as $105.33 as of April 24, while the WSJ simultaneously references front-month futures trading above $107. The bundle's $105.33 figure likely reflects a later-expiry futures contract, not the front-month, creating an apparent discrepancy. The report uses $105.33 as the headline Brent level, which is accurate per the bundle, but traders should note front-month was materially higher intraday.
- [Nasdaq composite stated in the report as having set a "fresh record close" alongside the S&P 500]: The Investopedia source confirms "the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite closing at new records" on April 22 (Wednesday). However, the data for Thursday (April 24) — the most recent close in the bundle — shows NASDAQ at 24,836.60 (+1.63%), which does not necessarily represent a new record if Thursday's session opened lower after Wednesday's records (as Investopedia confirms Thursday opened lower). The report's language implies the record closes were the most recent closes (April 24), but the sourced record closes appear to be from April 22.
- [Report states a "larger-than-expected US crude inventory build of 1.9 million barrels" as explanation for WTI's underperformance]: The data bundle confirms a 1.9 million barrel crude inventory build (via https://www.oilandgas360.com/weekly-gas-storage-04-17/), but that source is specifically about natural gas storage, not crude oil. The crude inventory build is mentioned in passing as a headline on that same page ("U.S. crude oil inventories increase by 1.9 million barrels"). The sourcing is technically present in the bundle but is not from a dedicated crude inventory report, so the "larger-than-expected" characterisation cannot be verified against consensus estimates from the bundle.
